questions.stem: This photographic plate compares the four labeled feeding stages of the tick vector shown. Which of these stages is the usual culprit in transmitting Lyme disease?

explanation.summary: The immature nymph is small and easily overlooked, so it typically remains attached long enough (36-48 h) to transmit Borrelia burgdorferi. The larger adult forms are usually noticed and removed before transmission.
explanation.whyCorrect: The immature nymph is small and easily overlooked, so it typically remains attached long enough (36-48 h) to transmit Borrelia burgdorferi. The larger adult forms are usually noticed and removed before transmission.

questions.stem: This expanding, target-like skin lesion with central clearing is considered pathognomonic for the early localized stage of which infection?

explanation.summary: Erythema migrans, the bull's-eye rash, appears in 70-80% of Lyme disease patients during stage 1 and is the hallmark early sign of Borrelia burgdorferi infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Erythema migrans, the bull's-eye rash, appears in 70-80% of Lyme disease patients during stage 1 and is the hallmark early sign of Borrelia burgdorferi infection.

questions.stem: This infographic illustrates blistering, disseminated, bull's-eye, and blue-red variants of a single rash. All of these presentations are caused by which organism?

explanation.summary: The figure shows the many morphologies the Lyme disease rash can take. All are manifestations of infection by the spirochete Borrelia burgdorferi; the rash may also be absent entirely.
explanation.whyCorrect: The figure shows the many morphologies the Lyme disease rash can take. All are manifestations of infection by the spirochete Borrelia burgdorferi; the rash may also be absent entirely.

questions.stem: This maculopapular rash, which begins on the wrists, ankles, palms, and soles before spreading toward the trunk, arises because the causative bacterium preferentially infects which cell type?

explanation.summary: Rickettsia rickettsii invades endothelial cells lining blood vessels, producing a vasculitis that manifests as the centripetal maculopapular (and later petechial) rash of Rocky Mountain spotted fever.
explanation.whyCorrect: Rickettsia rickettsii invades endothelial cells lining blood vessels, producing a vasculitis that manifests as the centripetal maculopapular (and later petechial) rash of Rocky Mountain spotted fever.
